Q1: My study routine was honestly kind of chaotic, even though I tried to be consistent. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd spend a lot of time doing practice problems and working through activities rather than just reading. I'd usually carve out time in the evenings after my extracurriculars, but with 16 online courses to juggle, I often felt like I was just trying to keep my head above water. Some weeks I'd put in solid hours, other weeks I'd barely manage to get through the essentials.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how anxious I was feeling about everything. I'd have these bursts of intense studying right before deadlines, then periods where I'd barely touch anything because I was just exhausted. The stress really got to me, and sometimes I'd avoid studying altogether because the anxiety was so overwhelming. I tried to have regular times, but life and my mental state kept interfering with those plans.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or a notification about something new being posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important because my anxiety made me paranoid about falling behind. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more regularly because there was something satisfying about completing those challenges. Other times I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I didn't accomplish much.
Q4: The gamified elements really helped make logging in feel more routine for me. When they introduced those task challenges, I found myself checking in more frequently to see my progress and earn points or badges. It became almost like a habit to log in during my morning coffee break. That structure gave me something tangible to work toward, which helped with my low motivation since just studying for the sake of studying felt overwhelming.
Q5: The interactive activities and practice exercises were most useful because I learn by doing, not by watching or reading. I also really valued the discussion forums because connecting with other students made me feel less alone in my struggles. The video content was helpful when I could actually focus on it, which wasn't always the case given my stress levels. The gamified tasks were surprisingly effective at keeping me engaged when my motivation was rock bottom.
Q6: I often skipped the longer reading materials and some of the supplementary videos because they felt passive and my mind would wander. With my anxiety being so high, sitting through long content without doing anything with my hands made me restless. I'd tell myself I'd come back to them later but rarely did. Some resources just felt like busy work, and when you're managing so many courses, you learn to prioritize what directly impacts your grades.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate results or application of what I was learning. The gamified tasks were great because I got instant feedback and could track my progress visually. Anything that let me practice and make mistakes without high stakes felt valuable. If something was just information dumping without interaction, I'd lose interest quickly because my brain needs that hands-on engagement to stay focused.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford, honestly. When I was drowning in required work and my anxiety was through the roof, optional stuff got pushed aside no matter how interesting it seemed. But when I did have the mental space, optional activities sometimes felt more enjoyable because there was no pressure attached. The gamified optional tasks were an exception though—they felt rewarding enough that I'd do them even when stressed.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened away from the LMS. I'd download materials and work through them on paper because physically writing and highlighting helps me retain information better. I also used external YouTube videos and practice sites that explained concepts in different ways. My textbooks got a lot of use too, especially when I needed to understand something more deeply than the course materials covered.
Q10: There were many times I'd be at my desk for hours working through problems on paper or making physical flashcards, none of which showed up anywhere online. I'd also study with classmates in person, quizzing each other and working through difficult concepts together. Sometimes I'd watch a video once on the LMS, then replay it in my head while practicing offline. My peer interactions number doesn't capture all the study sessions I had face-to-face with friends from class.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd open a PDF or video once to download or take notes, then work from those materials for days without logging back in. My notes became my primary study tool, so the LMS was really just a starting point for gathering resources. I'd screenshot important diagrams and have them on my phone to review during commutes. The initial access was just the beginning of a much longer offline engagement with the material.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety became so overwhelming that I could barely function, let alone engage with coursework. Around midterms, I hit a wall where the stress just paralyzed me, and my attendance dropped significantly. When multiple deadlines piled up across my courses, I'd feel so panicked that I'd sometimes just shut down and avoid everything. Those were the darkest moments, and the course felt impossible to face.
Q13: The workload was genuinely crushing at times, managing 16 courses while dealing with high anxiety and low resilience. Fatigue became a constant companion, and there were days I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen unable to process anything. My extracurriculars added another layer of time pressure, and sometimes I had to choose between attending a live session or taking care of my mental health. Confusion about course content would spike my anxiety, creating this vicious cycle where I'd avoid the very things that could help me understand better.
Q14: Many times I'd be scrolling through materials with my eyes glazed over, technically accessing content but not absorbing anything. My mind would be racing with worry about other deadlines or just general anxiety noise. I'd watch videos and realize at the end that I couldn't recall a single thing because I was so stressed. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actual learning.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when they felt overwhelming or when my anxiety convinced me I'd fail anyway. Large assignments would loom over me for weeks while I'd tackle smaller, less important tasks just to feel productive. Sometimes I'd avoid discussion posts because the idea of putting my thoughts out there for others to judge triggered my anxiety. I knew these things were important, but the emotional barrier felt insurmountable in the moment.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that could cut through my avoidance patterns. I'd have these intense cramming sessions right before something was due, which wasn't healthy but got things done. The gamified tasks with their mini-deadlines actually helped spread out my engagement more than traditional big deadlines. Without a deadline, I'd tell myself I had time and then suddenly it was too late—my low motivation made it hard to work ahead.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me because of my anxiety. Positive feedback would give me a temporary boost and motivate me to engage more. But critical feedback, even when constructive, could send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for days. I started checking feedback only when I felt emotionally prepared for it, which sometimes meant delays in adjusting my approach.
Q18: The course structure was a mixed bag for me. The gamified elements really helped because they broke things into manageable chunks with clear rewards. But the sheer volume of content across all my courses made it hard to keep up with any single one consistently. When the structure was clear and predictable, my anxiety was lower and I could plan better. Unclear expectations or sudden changes would throw me off completely.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier—they gave me something concrete to work toward when motivation was low. Interactive elements that let me learn by doing matched my kinesthetic style perfectly. What made it harder was when there were long stretches without clear milestones or when content was purely passive. Also, any ambiguity in instructions would spike my anxiety and make me avoid the task entirely.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and keeps checking the LMS obsessively without actually being able to focus on completing work. You might be logging in constantly, watching videos, reading discussion posts, but the anxiety prevents you from actually producing anything. It's like being stuck in preparation mode forever because starting the actual work feels too scary. I've definitely had weeks like that where my activity was high but my output was minimal.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. A lot of my real learning happened offline with downloaded materials, handwritten notes, and study groups. Someone could have low LMS activity but be deeply engaged with the content through other means. I think it also happens when students are efficient—they know exactly what they need, grab it quickly, and do the heavy lifting elsewhere. The LMS doesn't capture the hours I spent with flashcards or practicing problems on paper.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're most active, how often you engage, and what resources you access. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement—whether you're actually learning or just anxiously clicking around. They can't capture offline study, emotional state, or the depth of understanding you're developing. They also miss the human connections, like when a peer explains something in a way that finally makes it click during an in-person conversation.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting genuine effort into understanding it, not just completing tasks. For me, it's when I'm actively working through problems, asking questions, and connecting new information to what I already know. It's also about feeling some sense of investment in the course community and my own growth. True engagement feels different from just going through the motions to survive—it's when learning feels meaningful even if it's hard.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much my anxiety affected every interaction with the course, how I'd sometimes cry before logging in or feel paralyzed by stress. It wouldn't capture the support I got from friends and family that kept me going, or the personal victories of just showing up on days when everything felt impossible. It also misses how the gamified interventions genuinely shifted my mood and helped me feel more capable. My journey through this course was as much emotional as academic, and data can't capture that struggle or resilience.
